skillName: Mental strategies for addition and subtraction
misconceptionSummary: Students always use column methods mentally, losing track of carries.

steps.narration: When adding a near-multiple of ten or a hundred, round it up then adjust. Adding ninety-nine is quicker as adding one hundred then subtracting one. One step instead of carrying digits.
Partition both numbers into hundreds, tens, and ones, then add each group separately. Add the hundreds, then the tens, then the ones, and combine. No columns needed.

For subtractions where the numbers are close, count up from the smaller to the larger. From six ninety-eight to seven hundred is two; from seven hundred to seven thirty-four is thirty-four. Total difference: thirty-six.
Thirty-six. Counting up from six ninety-eight to seven thirty-four in two hops is far faster than a written subtraction.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: always setting up column subtraction for calculations like 734 − 698. When numbers are close, counting up is faster and less error-prone.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Choose your strategy based on the numbers. Near-multiples suit rounding and adjusting; close numbers suit counting up; large partitioned numbers suit splitting.
